food_name: Ban Mian, Dry (Hand-Torn Wheat Noodles with Minced Pork, Mushroom, Anchovy Broth and Poached Egg)
aliases: Ban Mian (dry — hand-torn wheat noodles, minced pork, mushroom, anchovy broth, poached egg)

english: Ban Mian (dry — hand-torn wheat noodles, minced pork, mushroom, anchovy broth, poached egg)

food_subgroup: Hand-torn or hand-cut irregular flat wheat noodles, tossed dry with minced pork, mushroom, black vinegar, and poached egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Ban Mian. Closest parent is a wheat noodle soup dish (FOODON:03315595). The dry tossed variant with minced pork and poached egg is not represented.
ontology.foodex2_description: Singapore Ban Mian — hand-torn wheat noodles with minced pork, mushroom, anchovy broth, and poached egg (dry version); closest_parent match under composite wheat noodle dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Ban Mian dry. A0BDF used as closest parent for composite wheat noodle dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept exists for Ban Mian or equivalent Singapore hawker noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of Ban Mian dry is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g hand-torn wheat noodles, 130 g toppings (minced pork, mushroom, egg, greens), and 120 g side anchovy broth.

nutrient_source.title: Ban Mian — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for Ban Mian as a composite Singapore hawker dish. Values for copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy using component-level data for wheat noodles, pork, shiitake mushroom, and egg, as these micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB Foodo entry. All core macros and primary micronutrients are sourced directly from HPB Foodo.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — fresh egg wheat noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Ban Mian dry. GI of 58 is estimated from fresh egg wheat noodles (GI ~55–62) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The presence of protein (pork, egg) and fat (egg yolk, pork) in the composite dish is expected to moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 69.75) / 100 ≈ 40.5 for a 450 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from anchovy broth, dark soy sauce dressing, and seasoned minced pork
hypertension.guidance: A 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,890 mg sodium — approaching the WHO single-day limit of 2,000 mg; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce seasoning, avoid drinking the full side broth, and consider this dish an occasional rather than daily meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (58) wheat noodles with glycaemic buffering from co-ingested protein and fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The moderate GI of hand-torn wheat noodles (~58) combined with substantial protein from pork and egg provides meaningful glycaemic buffering; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should eat the egg and pork before the noodles (food-order sequencing), request a smaller noodle portion, and note that black vinegar (acetic acid) in the dry dressing has documented glucose-attenuating effects.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) and high sodium (420 mg/100 g) offset by beneficial nutrients from egg, mushroom, and leafy greens
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat is relatively low at 1.2 g/100 g and cholesterol is 75 mg/100 g; however, the high sodium load (1,890 mg per 450 g serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern, and patients with CVD should limit broth intake, request less soy sauce, and balance this meal with low-sodium choices throughout the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrients from shiitake mushroom, egg, black vinegar, and leafy greens
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Shiitake mushrooms contribute beta-glucan and vitamin D2; the poached egg provides selenium and vitamin B12; black vinegar supplies acetic acid and polyphenols with documented anti-inflammatory properties; leafy greens provide folate and vitamin C — collectively making this dish supportive for patients with rheumatoid arthritis when sodium is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ban Mian (板面, literally 'flat noodles' or 'board noodles') is a beloved Singapore hawker noodle dish rooted in Hakka Chinese culinary tradition. Its defining feature is the hand-made wheat noodles — torn or cut by the hawker directly into the boiling pot to create irregular, rough-edged pieces with a pleasingly rustic, slightly chewy texture. The dry version (gān lāo / 干捞) is the most popular: cooked noodles are tossed with minced pork braised in a savoury soy-garlic sauce, sliced rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, and black vinegar, then served alongside a separate bowl of clear golden ikan bilis (dried anchovy) broth containing a gently poached egg and blanched spinach or chye sim. The broth is the soul of the dish — clean, pure, and anchovy-sweet, complementing the dry noodles without competing. Nutritionally, Ban Mian dry provides 128 kcal per 100 g with 8.5 g protein, 4.5 g fat, and 15.5 g carbohydrate. The poached egg and shiitake mushrooms together contribute vitamin D (0.5 mcg/100 g), selenium (12 mcg/100 g), and beta-glucan. Sodium is elevated at 420 mg/100 g, making broth management important for hypertensive patients. The moderate GI (~58) from fresh egg wheat noodles, combined with protein and fat co-ingestion, produces a moderate glycaemic response suitable for most Type 2 Diabetes management plans when portion size is controlled.

confidence_reason: Core macros and primary micronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database using fresh egg wheat noodles as the closest approved analogue. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ban Mian dry as a composite hawker dish is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in HPB Foodo. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per HPB Foodo source data for this dish. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on fresh egg wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Ban Mian dry exists in the published literature as of April 2026. Glycemic load of 40.5 calculated for a 450 g serving (69.75 g carbohydrate). Copper (0.06 mg/100 g) and manganese (0.08 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for wheat noodles, pork, shiitake mushroom, and egg; confidence 0.9. Vitamin K (12.0 mcg/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for leafy greens and egg; confidence 0.9. The dish is not certified halal as pork is a primary ingredient. Only one default serving size is recommended: 450 g (1 plate with side broth); the 380 g bowl variant is retained as an alternate portion.
